semantic_description: A right-leaning single decrease that merges two stitches into one by knitting them together. The second stitch overlaps the first, creating a line that leans to the right on the right side of the fabric. The most common right-leaning decrease in knitting. Paired with SSK for symmetrical shaping.

written_forms: knit the next two stitches together
insert needle through next 2 stitches together knitwise, wrap yarn, pull through both
knit 2 together
ambiguity_notes: CYC standard is 'k2tog'. Both 'k2tog' and 'K2tog' are in active use. 'K2 tog' (with space) appears in older patterns.

rs: Insert right needle through the next 2 stitches on left needle together, knitwise (from left to right through both)
Wrap working yarn counterclockwise around right needle
Pull new loop through both stitches
Slide both old stitches off left needle

semantic_description: A left-leaning single decrease that merges two stitches into one. Two stitches are slipped individually knitwise to reorient them, returned to the left needle, then knit together through the back loops. The first stitch overlaps the second, creating a line that leans to the left on the right side. The standard left-leaning counterpart to k2tog.

written_forms: slip 2 stitches individually knitwise, then knit them together through the back loops
slip, slip, knit slipped stitches together
slip 1 knitwise, slip 1 knitwise, insert left needle into front of both slipped stitches and knit together
slip 1 knitwise, slip 1 knitwise, insert left needle tip into fronts of both slipped stitches from left to right and knit together through back loops

vintage: sl 1, k1, psso was the original left-leaning decrease before SSK became standard

ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'ssk'. 'sl1-k1-psso' is the older form that 'ssk' replaced; some older Rowan/Debbie Bliss patterns still use it. 'SKP' in CYC is specifically slip 1 knitwise, k1, psso — equivalent result but mechanically distinct from the standard ssk slip-slip-knit. Both are left-leaning single decreases; treat 'SKP' as mapping to this operation cautiously.

Some publishers write 'ssk tbl', 'ssktbl', or 'ssk-tbl' to mean standard ssk, reflecting a misunderstanding that the tbl knit step is already implicit in the ssk instruction. Map these to `ssk` unless the pattern's legend explicitly describes a double-twisted result (in which case it is a rare Eastern European lace variant, not in this library).

rs: Slip the first stitch knitwise to right needle
Slip the second stitch knitwise to right needle
Insert left needle into the front of both slipped stitches (from left to right)
Knit the two stitches together from this position

canonical_name: Slip Knit Pass Slipped Stitch Over
semantic_description: An older method of creating a left-leaning single decrease. Slip one stitch knitwise, knit the next stitch, then pass the slipped stitch over the knit stitch. Produces the same left-leaning result as SSK but with slightly different tension. The traditional British left-leaning decrease, largely replaced by SSK in modern patterns.

alternate_names: slip one knit one pass slipped stitch over
slip one knit one pass over
written_forms: slip 1 knitwise, knit 1, pass slipped stitch over
sl 1, k1, pass slipped stitch over the knit stitch
slip, knit, pass slipped stitch over
slip 1 stitch knitwise to right needle, knit the next stitch, pass the slipped stitch over the knit stitch and off the needle
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists SKP as its own entry. SKP and ssk produce the same visual lean but use different mechanics. Some publishers use them interchangeably; others differentiate. Flag both as mapping to the same decrease direction (left-leaning single) but keep as distinct operations. 'skpo' is the UK form (slip, knit, pass over).

rs: Slip 1 stitch knitwise to right needle
Knit the next stitch
Using left needle tip, lift the slipped stitch over the knit stitch and off the right needle

canonical_name: Knit Two Together Through Back Loop
semantic_description: A left-leaning single decrease achieved by knitting two stitches together through their back loops. Produces a similar left-leaning result to SSK but with a slightly tighter, more twisted appearance. Some lace designers prefer this method for its neater result. The stitch is twisted in the process.

alternate_names: knit two together through back loop
knit two together through back loops
written_forms: knit 2 stitches together through their back loops
k2tog through back loop
insert right needle knitwise through the back loops of the next 2 stitches simultaneously and knit together
ambiguity_notes: This is a left-leaning twisted decrease. Some publishers (especially German/Austrian) use it where English-language publishers use ssk. The twisted result is visually similar to ssk but with a crossed stitch leg.

rs: Insert right needle through the back loops of the next 2 stitches (from right to left, behind the left needle)
Wrap yarn and pull through both stitches
Slide both old stitches off left needle

semantic_description: The purl-side equivalent of k2tog. Merges two stitches by purling them together. When viewed from the right side, it produces the same right-leaning decrease as k2tog. Used on wrong-side rows to maintain the shaping line established by k2tog on right-side rows.

written_forms: purl the next two stitches together
insert needle purlwise through next 2 stitches and purl together

rs: With yarn in front, insert right needle through the next 2 stitches purlwise (from right to left through both)
Wrap yarn and purl through both stitches
Slide both old stitches off left needle

semantic_description: The purl-side equivalent of SSK. Two stitches are slipped individually knitwise, returned to the left needle, and purled together through the back loops. Produces a left-leaning decrease when viewed from the right side. Used on wrong-side rows to maintain left-leaning shaping.

written_forms: slip 2 stitches individually knitwise, slip them back to left needle, purl together through back loops
slip, slip, purl slipped stitches together
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'ssp' as a left-leaning purl decrease. Distinct from 'SSP' (same letters, different caps conventions across publishers).
Some patterns use 'p2tog-tbl' instead of SSP — the result is similar but the mechanics differ slightly. p2tog-tbl is often easier to execute.

rs: Slip the first stitch knitwise
Slip the second stitch knitwise
Slip both stitches back to left needle in their new orientation
Purl the 2 stitches together through the back loops

canonical_name: Purl Two Together Through Back Loop
semantic_description: Purls two stitches together through their back loops, producing a left-leaning decrease when viewed from the right side. An alternative to SSP that some knitters find easier to execute. The result is nearly identical to SSP.

alternate_names: purl two together through back loop
written_forms: purl 2 stitches together through their back loops
insert needle purlwise through the back loops of the next 2 stitches simultaneously and purl together

rs: With yarn in front, insert right needle through the back loops of the next 2 stitches from left to right
Wrap yarn and purl through both stitches
Slide both old stitches off left needle

semantic_description: A centered double decrease that reduces three stitches to one with the center stitch on top, creating a vertically aligned decrease with no lean. Slip two stitches together knitwise, knit one, then pass the two slipped stitches over. The center stitch dominates visually. The standard centered decrease for lace patterns and symmetrical shaping.

alternate_names: slip 2 knit 1 pass 2 slipped stitches over
written_forms: slip 2 stitches together knitwise, knit 1, pass the 2 slipped stitches over the knit stitch
sl2tog kwise, k1, pass both slipped stitches over
slip 2 as if to k2tog, k1, pass slipped stitches over
ambiguity_notes: 'cdd' (centered/central double decrease) and 's2kp' (slip 2 together knitwise, k1, pass 2 slipped sts over) are the two most common forms. CYC lists 'S2KP2'. Some publishers use 'cdd', others 's2kp'. All produce a vertically stacked, left-center leaning result. 'SSSP' (CYC) is a different 3-stitch decrease — do not conflate.
Some publishers write 's2kp' and others 's2kp2' — the trailing '2' indicates passing 2 slipped stitches over. Both refer to the same operation.
The two stitches should be slipped TOGETHER (as if to k2tog), not individually. Slipping individually reorients the stitches and changes the appearance.

'S2KP' / 's2kp' is notationally ambiguous: CYC and JIS use it as the centered double decrease abbreviation, but many knitters read the letters literally as 'slip-slip-knit-pass' which is left-leaning (see op `s2kp`). Use 'S2KP2' / 'CDD' for unambiguous centered-double-decrease references. If a pattern writes bare 's2kp', consult its legend.

rs: Insert right needle into the next 2 stitches as if to k2tog (together, knitwise) and slip them to right needle
Knit the next stitch
Using left needle, pass both slipped stitches over the knit stitch and off the right needle

semantic_description: A centered 3-to-1 double decrease worked through the back loop: slip 2 stitches together through the back loops, knit 1, then pass the 2 slipped stitches over. The result is centered with no lean (like CDD), but the flanking stitches are twisted, producing a faux-cable / textured central column rather than the smooth column of standard CDD. Used in textured lace and Estonian-influenced charts where the designer wants extra definition around the centered decrease.

alternate_names: central double decrease through back loop
written_forms: slip 2 stitches together through the back loop, knit 1, pass the 2 slipped stitches over the knit stitch
sl2tog tbl, k1, p2sso
ambiguity_notes: The chart symbol (centered peaked triangle) is visually identical to standard cdd — disambiguate via legend text. If a pattern's legend specifies 'sl2 tbl k1 p2sso' or 'tbl' anywhere in the decrease description, this is the operation; otherwise default to cdd.

Documented as a deliberate textural alternative to cdd by Tin Can Knits and several Estonian lace publishers. Mechanically distinct from cdd because the back-loop slip leaves the flanking stitches twisted.

rs: Insert right needle into the next 2 stitches together through the back loops and slip them to the right needle
Knit the next stitch
Using left needle, pass both slipped stitches over the knit stitch and off the right needle

canonical_name: Slip 1 Knit 2 Together Pass Slipped Stitch Over
semantic_description: A left-leaning double decrease. Slip one stitch, knit the next two together, then pass the slipped stitch over. Reduces three stitches to one with a slight left lean. Similar to CDD but the visual emphasis differs — the slipped stitch travels over the k2tog, putting the leftmost original stitch on top.

alternate_names: slip knit 2 together pass slipped stitch over
written_forms: slip 1, knit 2 together, pass slipped stitch over the k2tog
slip 1 knitwise, k2tog, pass slipped stitch over
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'SK2P' as slip 1 knitwise, k2tog, pass slipped stitch over — this is a left-leaning double decrease (not centered). Distinct from cdd/s2kp which is centered. Some patterns confuse these — the mechanical difference: sk2p slips only 1 stitch, cdd slips 2 together.

rs: Slip 1 stitch knitwise to right needle
Knit the next 2 stitches together (k2tog)
Using left needle, lift the slipped stitch over the k2tog stitch and off the needle

semantic_description: A right-leaning double decrease that merges three stitches into one by knitting them all together. The rightmost stitch dominates visually. Produces a strong right-leaning line. The right-leaning counterpart to SSSK or SK2P.

written_forms: knit the next three stitches together
insert needle through 3 stitches knitwise and knit together
ambiguity_notes: Right-leaning double decrease. CYC does not list it explicitly but it follows the k2tog pattern.

rs: Insert right needle through the next 3 stitches on left needle together, knitwise
Wrap yarn and pull through all 3 stitches
Slide all 3 old stitches off left needle

semantic_description: A left-leaning double decrease. Three stitches are slipped individually knitwise, returned to the left needle, and knit together through the back loops. The left-leaning counterpart to k3tog. Less common than CDD or SK2P.

written_forms: slip 3 stitches individually knitwise, then knit all 3 together through the back loops
slip, slip, slip, knit slipped stitches together
slip 1 knitwise, slip 1 knitwise, slip 1 knitwise, insert left needle from left to right through fronts of all 3 slipped stitches and knit together
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'sssk' as a double left-leaning decrease. The 'SSSK' form (uppercase) is occasionally seen.

rs: Slip 3 stitches individually knitwise to right needle
Insert left needle into the front of all 3 slipped stitches
Knit all 3 together from this position

semantic_description: The purl-side equivalent of k3tog. Purls three stitches together, producing a right-leaning double decrease when viewed from the right side. Used on wrong-side rows.

written_forms: purl the next three stitches together

rs: With yarn in front, insert right needle through the next 3 stitches purlwise
Wrap yarn and purl through all 3 stitches
Slide all 3 old stitches off left needle

canonical_name: Purl Three Together Through Back Loop
semantic_description: A purl-side double decrease that merges three stitches into one by inserting the needle through the back loops of all three stitches and purling them together. Produces a right-leaning decrease when viewed from the right side, identical in lean to p3tog, but with all stitch legs twisted due to the through-back-loop execution. The result is a tighter, more textured decrease than p3tog, often used in lace traditions that deliberately exploit twisted stitches as a decorative element (Estonian and German lace in particular). The execution is physically demanding: the needle must navigate behind three stacked stitches simultaneously, which requires loose tension and some practice. It is the three-stitch extension of p2tog_tbl and shares its mechanical logic. It is distinct from sssp (which achieves a left-leaning purl double decrease via slipping and back-loop purling) — the lean here is right, not left.

alternate_names: purl three together through back loop
written_forms: purl 3 stitches together through their back loops
insert needle through the back loops of the next 3 stitches simultaneously, purlwise, and purl together
p3tog tbl: with yarn in front, insert right needle through back loops of next 3 sts from left to right and purl all 3 together
ambiguity_notes: p3tog_tbl is visually similar to p3tog on a chart — differentiate only by legend. If a pattern's legend does not explicitly include 'tbl', 'through back loop', or the equivalent German/Estonian notation, default to p3tog.

Some knitters substitute p3tog for p3tog_tbl in practice because the back-loop entry across three stitches is awkward. The stitch count is identical; the visual difference is a slightly tighter, crossed texture visible only on the RS.

rs: With yarn in front, insert right needle from left to right through the back loops of the next 3 stitches simultaneously (needle passes behind the left needle through all three back loops)
Wrap yarn and pull through all 3 stitches in one motion
Slide all 3 original stitches off the left needle

semantic_description: The purl-side equivalent of SSSK. Produces a left-leaning double decrease when viewed from the right side. Rarely encountered but used in fully charted patterns with WS shaping.

written_forms: slip 3 stitches individually knitwise, return to left needle, purl together through back loops
slip 3 stitches individually knitwise, return all 3 to left needle purlwise, purl all 3 together through their back loops
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'SSPP2' for a different centered purl decrease. 'sssp' is the purl equivalent of sssk (3-stitch left purl decrease). Not to be confused with the CYC 'SSPP2' entry which is centered.

rs: Slip 3 stitches individually knitwise
Return all 3 to left needle in new orientation
Purl all 3 together through the back loops

semantic_description: The wrong-side equivalent of the central double decrease (CDD). Produces a centered, vertically aligned double decrease when viewed from the right side. Used when a centered decrease line must continue on wrong-side rows.

written_forms: slip 2 stitches together purlwise, purl 1, pass slipped stitches over
slip 2 stitches together purlwise to right needle, purl 1, pass both slipped stitches over the purled stitch
ambiguity_notes: The purl-side centered double decrease. 'sp2p' (slip 1, p2tog, pass slipped st over) is one mechanical implementation. CYC lists 'SSPP2' as a similar centered purl decrease — 'SSPP2' is: slip 2 sts as if to p2tog-tbl, purl 1, pass 2 slipped sts over. These may or may not be identical depending on the publisher.

rs: Slip 2 stitches together as if to p2tog to right needle
Purl the next stitch
Pass both slipped stitches over the purled stitch

semantic_description: A large right-leaning decrease that reduces four stitches to one. Equivalent to k4tog — knitting four stitches together. Used in dramatic shaping, star-top decreases on hats, and some lace motifs. Creates a strong rightward lean.

written_forms: knit 4 stitches together
insert needle through next 4 stitches knitwise and knit together

rs: Insert right needle through the next 4 stitches on left needle knitwise
Wrap yarn and pull through all 4
Slide all 4 old stitches off left needle

semantic_description: A large left-leaning decrease that reduces four stitches to one. Slip three stitches knitwise individually, knit one, pass all three slipped stitches over. The mirror image of k4tog.

written_forms: slip 3 stitches individually knitwise, knit 1, pass all 3 slipped stitches over
sssk-k variant: work sssk then k2tog, or equivalent publisher-defined technique
ambiguity_notes: No universally standard single abbreviation. 'sl3-k1-p3sso' is descriptive. Some patterns write 'sl3 kwise, k1, p3sso'. 'dec4l' is a reasonable internal shorthand.

rs: Slip 3 stitches individually knitwise to right needle
Knit the next stitch
Pass all 3 slipped stitches over the knit stitch

semantic_description: A large centered decrease that reduces four stitches to one with the center stitch on top. A vertically-aligned quadruple decrease. Used in star motifs and dramatic lace decreases.

written_forms: slip 3 stitches together, knit 1, pass slipped stitches over — centered variant
ambiguity_notes: No standard abbreviation. These are typically pattern-defined.

rs: Slip 2 stitches together knitwise
Slip 1 stitch knitwise
Reorder so center stitch is on top
Knit 1 (the center stitch)
Pass remaining stitches over

semantic_description: A dramatic right-leaning decrease reducing five stitches to one. Knit five stitches together. Used in star-top shaping, dramatic lace motifs, and entrelac endings.

written_forms: knit 5 stitches together

rs: Insert right needle through the next 5 stitches knitwise
Wrap yarn and pull through all 5
Slide all 5 old stitches off

semantic_description: A dramatic left-leaning decrease reducing five stitches to one. The mirror of k5tog.

written_forms: slip 4 stitches individually knitwise, knit 1, pass all 4 slipped stitches over
sssk2 variant: work sssk then k2tog twice, or equivalent publisher-defined technique
ambiguity_notes: No standard abbreviation. Pattern-defined in most sources.

rs: Slip 4 stitches individually knitwise
Knit the next stitch
Pass all 4 slipped stitches over

semantic_description: Reduces six stitches to one with a right lean. Rare but used in some star motifs and dramatic shaping.

written_forms: knit 6 stitches together

rs: Insert right needle through the next 6 stitches knitwise
Wrap yarn and pull through all 6

semantic_description: Reduces six stitches to one with a left lean. The mirror of k6tog.

written_forms: slip 5 stitches individually knitwise, knit 1, pass all 5 slipped stitches over

rs: Slip 5 stitches individually knitwise
Knit the next stitch
Pass all 5 slipped stitches over

semantic_description: Reduces seven stitches to one with a right lean. Very rare, used in some star and flower motifs.

written_forms: knit 7 stitches together

rs: Insert right needle through the next 7 stitches knitwise
Wrap yarn and pull through all 7

semantic_description: Reduces seven stitches to one with a left lean.

written_forms: slip 6 stitches individually knitwise, knit 1, pass all 6 slipped stitches over

rs: Slip 6 stitches individually knitwise
Knit the next stitch
Pass all 6 slipped stitches over

semantic_description: A generic left-leaning single decrease symbol. Unlike exact operations such as SSK, SKP, or k2tog tbl, this entry represents a chart or legend item that specifies only the lean direction, not the exact mechanics. It is appropriate when the source distinguishes a generic left-slanting decrease from named methods.

written_forms: work a left-leaning decrease as specified in the legend
decrease two stitches to one with a left slant
ambiguity_notes: Generic chart placeholder only. Should always resolve to a specific operation (ssk, sssk, skp) per pattern legend. Use only when the pattern gives a 'left decrease' symbol without specifying method.

rs: Work the left-leaning decrease method specified by the pattern or legend
Common realizations include SSK, SKP, or k2tog tbl depending on the designer's convention

semantic_description: A generic right-leaning single decrease symbol. Unlike exact operations such as k2tog, this entry captures sources that specify only a right slant without committing to a particular method. It is the mirror-image counterpart to a generic left-leaning decrease.

written_forms: work a right-leaning decrease as specified in the legend
decrease two stitches to one with a right slant
ambiguity_notes: Generic placeholder. Always prefer k2tog or specific form.

rs: Work the right-leaning decrease method specified by the pattern or legend
Common realizations include k2tog or a source-specific right-slanting decrease

semantic_description: A generic decrease symbol whose exact lean or method is not specified. This represents a legend-level decrease instruction rather than a named operation such as k2tog or SSK. It should only be used when the source truly leaves the decrease unspecified.

written_forms: work a decrease as specified in the legend
ambiguity_notes: CYC lists 'dec' as a general term. 'dc' in a knitting context is fine but can look like crochet double crochet — flag if pattern may be mixed craft. Use only as last resort when no directional information is given.
'dc' also means double crochet in crochet patterns; treat as ambiguous and resolve by context

rs: Follow the pattern's legend for the specific decrease method

semantic_description: A generic purl-side decrease symbol. It identifies a decrease worked purlwise or on a purl ground without fully specifying whether the exact method is p2tog, SSP, or another purl decrease. It is more precise than a fully generic decrease, but less specific than a named purl decrease method.

written_forms: work a purl decrease as specified in the legend
ambiguity_notes: Generic purl decrease placeholder. 'pd' is rare and low-collision in pure knitting context.

rs: Work the purl decrease method specified by the pattern

semantic_description: A generic decrease worked through the back loop or in a twisted orientation. This captures source symbols that indicate the decrease is twisted or back-looped without fully specifying whether the underlying method is knitwise or purlwise.

written_forms: work the decrease through the back loop as specified in the legend
ambiguity_notes: Generic twisted decrease placeholder. Specific forms (k2tog-tbl, p2tog-tbl) should be preferred when known.

rs: Work the named decrease through the back loop or crossed orientation specified by the pattern

semantic_description: A generic right-leaning three-to-one decrease symbol. Unlike k3tog, this entry captures sources that identify the stitch-count change and lean direction without committing to one exact method. Marnen's JIS-derived symbol list and StitchMastery both distinguish a generic 3-to-1 right decrease from named methods.

written_forms: work a three-to-one decrease with a right lean as specified in the legend
ambiguity_notes: 'k3tog' is the most common form for 3-to-1 right. 'dec3r' is an internal convenience abbreviation.

rs: Work the right-leaning 3-to-1 decrease method specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: A generic left-leaning three-to-one decrease symbol. It captures the left slant and the 3-to-1 stitch change without forcing a specific method such as SSSK or SK2P. This is distinct from exact named operations and is the mirror of a generic 3-to-1 right decrease.

written_forms: work a three-to-one decrease with a left lean as specified in the legend
ambiguity_notes: 'sssk' is the most common form for 3-to-1 left. 'dec3l' is internal.

rs: Work the left-leaning 3-to-1 decrease method specified in the pattern legend

written_forms: compound decrease where two outer stitches decrease toward the outside; method varies by designer, consult legend
ambiguity_notes: 'Bunny ears outward' (also called 'outward double decrease') is the compound k2tog-then-ssk on the same stitch. Not in CYC. 'BEO' follows the pattern of the informal community name. Pattern-defined in most cases.

semantic_description: Bunny Ears Outward — also called bunny ears outward, double decrease outward, outward double decrease. compound decrease where two outer stitches decrease toward the outside; method varies by designer, consult legend

rs: compound decrease where two outer stitches decrease toward the outside; method varies by designer, consult legend

written_forms: compound decrease where two outer stitches decrease toward the inside; method varies, consult legend
ambiguity_notes: Same situation as bunny_ears_outward but mirrored (ssk-then-k2tog). Community term not in CYC.

semantic_description: Bunny Ears Inward — also called bunny ears inward, double decrease inward, inward double decrease. compound decrease where two outer stitches decrease toward the inside; method varies, consult legend

rs: compound decrease where two outer stitches decrease toward the inside; method varies, consult legend

canonical_name: Knit Three Together Through Back Loop

alternate_names: knit three together through back loop
written_forms: insert needle knitwise through back loops of next 3 stitches simultaneously and knit together

semantic_description: Knit Three Together Through Back Loop — also called knit three together through back loop, twisted k3tog. insert needle knitwise through back loops of next 3 stitches simultaneously and knit together

rs: insert needle knitwise through back loops of next 3 stitches simultaneously and knit together

written_forms: slip 4 stitches together knitwise, knit 1, pass all 4 slipped stitches over
ambiguity_notes: Pattern-defined. No standard abbreviation.

semantic_description: Decrease 5 to 1 (Centered) — also called centered quintuple decrease, 5-stitch centered decrease, 5-to-1 center. slip 4 stitches together knitwise, knit 1, pass all 4 slipped stitches over

rs: slip 4 stitches together knitwise, knit 1, pass all 4 slipped stitches over

written_forms: work 6 stitches together into 1 with a centered lean as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: Decrease 6 to 1 (Centered) — also called centered 6-stitch decrease, 6-to-1 center. work 6 stitches together into 1 with a centered lean as specified in the pattern legend

rs: work 6 stitches together into 1 with a centered lean as specified in the pattern legend

written_forms: work 7 stitches together into 1 with a centered lean as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: Decrease 7 to 1 (Centered) — also called centered 7-stitch decrease, 7-to-1 center. work 7 stitches together into 1 with a centered lean as specified in the pattern legend

rs: work 7 stitches together into 1 with a centered lean as specified in the pattern legend

semantic_description: A centered double decrease from Estonian Muhu-island knitting tradition. The center stitch is slipped knitwise, the flanking stitches are worked together (typically through back loop), and the slipped stitch is passed over. Produces a 3-to-1 centered decrease with a distinctive stitch orientation used in Muhu colorwork and selvedge patterning.

written_forms: slip 1 knitwise, work flanking stitches together (often tbl), pass slipped stitch over
ambiguity_notes: Not in CYC. Estonian-specific. Usually defined in the pattern legend.

rs: Slip the center stitch knitwise
Work the two flanking stitches together (often tbl)
Pass the slipped center stitch over

semantic_description: An Estonian technique where a stitch is slipped on the current row and the corresponding decrease is completed on the following row. The deferred action creates a sharper diagonal than a same-row decrease. Appears in Estonian lace and border patterns.

written_forms: slip a stitch on this row; complete the decrease on the following row
ambiguity_notes: Not in CYC. Estonian-specific. Always defined in the pattern legend. Spans two rows mechanically.

rs: Slip a stitch on this row without working it
On the next row where this stitch is worked, complete the decrease as specified

semantic_description: A single right-leaning decrease. Knit 1 stitch, slip it back from the right needle to the left needle, pass the next stitch over it, and return the stitch to the right needle. Mechanically distinct from k2tog but produces a similar visual result. CYC-standard abbreviation 'ksp'.

written_forms: knit 1, slip stitch back to left needle, pass next stitch over, return stitch to right needle
ambiguity_notes: CYC-standard abbreviation. Produces a right-leaning single decrease similar to k2tog in appearance but with a different mechanical sequence. Note: don't confuse with 'skp' (slip-knit-pass) which is LEFT-leaning.

rs: Knit the first stitch normally
Slip the just-knit stitch back to the left needle
Pass the next stitch on the left needle over the slipped stitch and off
Return the remaining stitch to the right needle

semantic_description: Purl 4 stitches together as one, producing a right-leaning decrease as viewed from the right side (the RS counterpart of k4tog). Used on WS rows when a chart shows a right-leaning decrease symbol.

written_forms: purl 4 stitches together as one
ambiguity_notes: Right-leaning purl decrease. WS equivalent of k4tog — when a right-leaning decrease symbol appears on a WS row, this is the action worked.

rs: Purl 4 stitches together as one stitch

semantic_description: Purl 5 stitches together as one, producing a right-leaning decrease as viewed from the right side (the RS counterpart of k5tog). Used on WS rows when a chart shows a right-leaning decrease symbol.

written_forms: purl 5 stitches together as one
ambiguity_notes: Right-leaning purl decrease. WS equivalent of k5tog — when a right-leaning decrease symbol appears on a WS row, this is the action worked.

rs: Purl 5 stitches together as one stitch

semantic_description: Purl 6 stitches together as one, producing a right-leaning decrease as viewed from the right side (the RS counterpart of k6tog). Used on WS rows when a chart shows a right-leaning decrease symbol.

written_forms: purl 6 stitches together as one
ambiguity_notes: Right-leaning purl decrease. WS equivalent of k6tog — when a right-leaning decrease symbol appears on a WS row, this is the action worked.

rs: Purl 6 stitches together as one stitch

semantic_description: Purl 7 stitches together as one, producing a right-leaning decrease as viewed from the right side (the RS counterpart of k7tog). Used on WS rows when a chart shows a right-leaning decrease symbol.

written_forms: purl 7 stitches together as one
ambiguity_notes: Right-leaning purl decrease. WS equivalent of k7tog — when a right-leaning decrease symbol appears on a WS row, this is the action worked.

rs: Purl 7 stitches together as one stitch

semantic_description: A left-leaning 4-to-1 decrease worked on the WS row as the purl counterpart of dec_4_to_1_left. When a chart shows the left-leaning 4-to-1 decrease symbol on a WS row, this is the action worked.

written_forms: Work a left-leaning 4-to-1 purl decrease as specified in the legend.
ambiguity_notes: WS counterpart op — chart symbols that show this decrease on a WS row map here.

rs: Work the left-leaning 4-to-1 purl decrease per pattern legend.

semantic_description: A center-leaning 4-to-1 decrease worked on the WS row as the purl counterpart of dec_4_to_1_center. When a chart shows the center-leaning 4-to-1 decrease symbol on a WS row, this is the action worked.

written_forms: Work a center-leaning 4-to-1 purl decrease as specified in the legend.
ambiguity_notes: WS counterpart op — chart symbols that show this decrease on a WS row map here.

rs: Work the center-leaning 4-to-1 purl decrease per pattern legend.

canonical_name: Slip, P2tog, Pass Slipped Stitch Over
semantic_description: A centered-left-leaning double decrease worked on the WS: slip one purlwise, p2tog, pass the slipped stitch over. The purl counterpart of sk2p (sl1, k2tog, psso), worked when a chart shows an sk2p symbol on a WS row.

written_forms: sl1 purlwise, p2tog, psso
ambiguity_notes: WS counterpart of sk2p.

rs: Slip 1 purlwise, p2tog, pass the slipped stitch over.

semantic_description: A left-leaning 3-to-1 decrease read literally as 'slip-slip-knit-pass': slip 1 knitwise, slip 1 knitwise, knit 1, pass both slipped stitches over. The result is a left-leaning decrease distinct from CDD (which is centered). This op covers the literal letter-by-letter reading of 's2kp' some publishers use. CYC and JIS use 'S2KP' as an abbreviation for the centered CDD — if a pattern writes 'S2KP' without defining it, check the legend.

written_forms: slip 1 knitwise, slip 1 knitwise, knit 1, pass both slipped stitches over the knit stitch
ambiguity_notes: Bare 'S2KP' / 's2kp' is CYC's abbreviation for the centered CDD. This op covers the alternate reading where s2kp is parsed literally as 'slip slip knit pass' and produces a left-leaning result. Use the pattern's legend to disambiguate.

rs: Slip the next stitch knitwise to the right needle
Slip the next stitch knitwise to the right needle
Knit the next stitch
Pass both slipped stitches over the knit stitch together
